questions.question: In the following question, four words are given out of which one word is incorrectly spelt. Find the incorrectly spelt word.
questions.explanation: The correct spelling is Stampede.

questions.question: In the following questions, four words are given, out of which only one word is correctly spelt. Find the correctly spelt word.
questions.explanation: The correct spellings of the other words are: mountebank, apostate, and composure. A is the right answer.

questions.question: In the following question, a sentence has been given in Active/Passive Voice. Out of the four alternatives suggested, select the one which best expresses the same sentence in Passive/Active Voice. He plucked a red rose from the flowerbed in the garden.
options: A red rose is plucked by him from the flowerbed in the garden.
A red rose had been plucked by him from the flowerbed in the garden.
A red rose was plucked by him from the flowerbed in the garden.
A red rose was plucked by he from the flowerbed in the garden.
questions.explanation: The sentence is in active voice and in simple past tense (plucked). A is incorrect as it is in simple present tense (is plucked). B is incorrect as 'had been plucked' is in past perfect tense. D is incorrect as although the tense is consistent, but the subjective pronoun 'he' has been used in place of the objective pronoun 'him'. C is the right answer as 'was plucked' is in simple past tense.

questions.question: In the below question, sentence/passage is split into four parts and named P, Q, R & S. These four parts are not given in their proper order. Read the sentence/passage and find out which of the four combinations is correct. P. I indicated my small overnight case which was lying beside him on the bunk and said: 'If you open my case you'll find something in it for you. Q. We had less than five minutes before the train was due to depart. R. In that time, we stood talking for a little with the door half-closed, Kenrick facing the corridor. S. Then, he said that he better get out, or he might be carried to the Highlands.
questions.explanation: Sentences Q and R are a pair: Q tells us about the time left before the train departed, and R follows by telling us how that time was spent. Options B and D are eliminated. Sentence S must follow the pair QR, as it tells us what happened after those five minutes were over. Option A is eliminated. Option C is the right answer.

questions.question: In the below question, sentence/passage is split into four parts and named P, Q, R & S. These four parts are not given in their proper order. Read the sentence/passage and find out which of the four combinations is correct. P. His first feeling was disappointment. Q. It was not the fallen-on-evil-times look of the house - although that was evident; it was the sheer ugliness of it. R. Either it had been built too late to share in the grace of a graceful period, or the builder had lacked an architect's eye. S. He had used the idiom of the time, but it had apparently not been native to him.
questions.explanation: Sentences P and Q are a pair: P describes his feeling of disappointment, while Q builds up on it, giving us the reason behind it. Options B and C are eliminated. Sentences R and S are also a pair: S refers to an idiom used in the previous sentence. R uses the idiom 'architect's eye'. Thus, S will follow R. Option D is eliminated. Option A is the right answer.

questions.question: India is an ___________ programme of
questions.explanation: Option B is the right answer, as it fits most appropriately in the context of the passage. The blank is preceded by the article 'an', suggesting that only a word starting with vowel sound can fit in. This eliminates options A and C. 'fictitious' means unreal. 'contentious' means controversial, involving heated argument. 'ordinary' is eliminated as gives a meaning contrary to the tone of the sentence.

questions.question: will be for ___________ India for
questions.explanation: Option A is the right answer, as it fits most appropriately in the context of the passage. All others options are non-contextual. 'impairing' means to weaken or damage. 'devouring' means to consume.

questions.question: delivering good __________ to citizens
questions.explanation: We need a noun for this blank as only a noun/ pronoun can be qualified by an adjective (good). Option A and B are eliminated as they are verbs. Option C is eliminated as it is non-contextual. 'governance' means the action or manner of governing a state, organization, etc. 'garnering' means to gather or collect.

questions.question: has been ___________ by Department
questions.explanation: 'envisaged' means to foresee or predict. 'ensued' means to happen or occur afterwards or as a result. 'environed' means to surround or enclose. 'invested' means to devote or infuse. Only option A fits the context of the given statement.

questions.question: Technology and will _________ ministry of communications
questions.explanation: The sentence talks about how the programme will effect the different ministries. Option D is the right answer, as it fits most appropriately in the context of the passage. All other options are non-contextual. 'detract' means to diminish the worth or value of.

questions.question: Change the form from Direct to Indirect Speech or vice-versa Anand said, "We passed by a beautiful beach when we went on a trip to Auckland."
options: Anand said that they passed by a beautiful beach when they had gone on a trip to Auckland.
Anand said that they had passed by a beautiful beach when they had gone on a trip to Auckland.
Anand said that they had passed by a beautiful beach when they went on a trip to Auckland.
Anand told that they passed by a beautiful beach when they had gone on a trip to Auckland.
questions.explanation: The above sentence is in simple past. When converting from direct to indirect speech, simple past tense is replaced by past perfect tense. Additionally, the subjective pronoun 'we' will be replace by 'they'. This reflects in option b.

questions.question: Out of the four alternatives, choose the one which can be substituted for the given words/sentences and mark it. On who is excessively talkative, especially on trivial matters
questions.explanation: Taciturn is the one who talks less. Aggressive is ready or likely to attack or confront. Extrovert is an outgoing, socially confident person. Garrulous is a person having the habit of talking a lot, especially about things that are not important.

questions.question: Fill in the blanks Thus we begin to see why a few simple objects, like the sun, the sky, the dawn, and the night, should be ___________ in mythology by such a host of gods, goddesses, and heroes.
questions.explanation: Option A has the word 'liked', which doesn't fit into the context here. Option A is eliminated. Option B is eliminated, as it has the word 'personified' which means attributed human characteristics to something non-human. Here, the sentence is instead talking about a natural phenomenon being represented by figures. Option D is eliminated as it has the word 'incarnated', which is used for referring to a deity represented in human form. This is not the case here. Option C is the right answer, as it has the word 'represented' meaning depicted, which most is the most accurate fit in the sentence.

questions.question: Out of the four alternatives, choose the one which can be substituted for the given words/sentences. A person who is not present at the scene of crime, but helps another in committing the crime
questions.explanation: A person who is not present at the scene of crime, but helps another in committing it is called an Accessary. Synonyms are Accessory, Accomplice etc. Accusatory: To indicate that a person has done something wrong. Additory means additional or supplementary. Allegory: a story, poem, or picture with a hidden message or meaning.

questions.question: Out of the five alternatives, choose the one which best expresses the meaning of the given word. Quandary
questions.explanation: Quandary means a state of perplexity or uncertainty over what to do in a difficult situation. Synonyms are dilemma, plight, predicament.

questions.question: Out of the four alternatives, choose the one which best expresses the meaning of the given word. CATHARSIS
questions.explanation: Catharsis means providing psychological relief through the expression of strong emotions. Example: The removal of the ventilator from her face was really cathartic for my niece.

questions.question: Out of the four alternatives, choose the one which is opposite in meaning to the given word. VOUCHSAFE
questions.explanation: Vouchsafe means to give, grant or reveal something in a gracious manner. Example: When I got selected for my dream job, it felt to me like a blessing vouchsafed from heaven. So, the antonym from the given alternatives is 'refuse'.

questions.question: Choose the word opposite in meaning to the given word. Inveterate
questions.explanation: Inveterate means "firmly established over time". Hence the word opposite in meaning is "brief" or "short-lived"

questions.question: Sentences given with blanks to be filled in with an appropriate word(s). Four alternatives are suggested for each question. Choose the correct alternative out of the four. Neither the students nor the teacher ___ about what happens on this trip.
questions.explanation: We must use Simple present tense as the other part of the sentence is also in Simple Present Tense. So, C and D can be eliminated. Now, in "Neither..Nor" sentence formation, the subject is the one which is nearest to the verb. Hence, "teacher" is the subject in this sentence which is singular and hence we will use Singular verb. Hence, B is correct.

questions.question: Four alternative are given for the bold idiom/phrase. Choose the alternative which best expresses the meaning of the idiom/phrase. To hold something in leash
questions.explanation: The idiom means to control or restrain

questions.question: Four alternative are given for the bold idiom/phrase. Choose the alternative which best expresses the meaning of the idiom/phrase. To be at logger heads
options: To be at enmity or strife
To tax one's mind and body
questions.explanation: The idiom means to strongly disagree with someone

questions.question: In the given sentence, a part of the sentence is bracketed. Below are given alternatives to the bracketed part, which may improve the sentence. Choose the correct alternative. In case no improvement is needed, choose your answer is (D). What (do agonise me most) is not this criticism, but the trival reason behind it.
questions.explanation: The simple present tense of the verb 'agonises' must be used here in place of the present participle form 'agonising'. The verb form should then be followed by the objective pronoun 'me'. The correct usage is 'agonises me the most'

questions.question: A sentence is given, divided into parts. Some of the parts are grammatically correct and some are incorrect. Find out which part of the sentence has an error and mark that part. If there is no error mark option (d) as your answer. In spite of the doctor's stern warning (A)/ he continued taking sugar (B)/ in the tea. (C)/ No error (D)
questions.explanation: The sentence is correct as it is.

questions.question: In the following question, a sentence is divided into parts. Some part may contain grammatical error. If any part contains error then mark that option as the answer and if there is no error then mark "no error" as the answer. The recommendations of the committee (A)/ pertaining to the lowering of the (B)/ legal age of drinking were not accept. (C)/ No error (D)
questions.explanation: In the construction here, (were + verb), the past participle form of the verb "accepted" must be used. Option (c) is the correct choice.

questions.question: A sentence/a part of the sentence is bracketed. Four alternatives are given for the bracketed part which will improve the sentence. Choose the correct alternative and click the button corresponding to it. In case no improvement is needed, click the button corresponding to "No improvement". When it comes to his love of sports, he (takes off) his father.
questions.explanation: The correct phrasal verb to be used here is 'takes after', which means to inherit something from someone. 'Takes from' holds no meaning here. 'Takes by' is ungrammatical. 'Takes off' means to leave. C is the right answer.
